summary: Judith Butler argues that gender is not an innate, stable identity or an expression of an internal essence, but rather a 'performative accomplishment' constituted through the stylized repetition of bodily acts over time. Drawing on phenomenology and feminist theory, she asserts that the illusion of a natural gender is maintained by social sanctions and taboos, and that recognizing gender as performative opens up possibilities for subversive repetition and cultural transformation.

principles.statement: Gender is a performative accomplishment, not a stable identity.
principles.rationale: Gender is created through a stylized repetition of acts over time, which creates the compelling illusion of an abiding gendered self. It is not a locus of agency from which acts proceed, but the result of the acts themselves.
principles.source_quote: gender is in no way a stable identity or locus of agency from which various acts proceede; rather, it is an identity tenuously constituted in time-an identity instituted through a stylized repetition of acts.

principles.statement: Gender is a strategy of survival regulated by punitive social consequences.
principles.rationale: Society demands discrete genders to 'humanize' individuals. Because gender is not a natural fact, it must be compelled under duress; those who fail to perform their gender correctly face marginalization and punishment.
principles.source_quote: Hence, as a strategy of survival, gender is a performance with clearly punitive consequences. Discrete genders are part of what 'humanizes' individuals within contemporary culture; indeed, those who fail to do their gender right are regularly punished.

principles.statement: Gender attributes do not express a pre-existing interior identity; they constitute it.
principles.rationale: The popular idea that acts express a 'true' gender core is a regulatory fiction. The acts themselves create the illusion of the interiority they supposedly express, meaning there is no 'true' or 'false' gender.
principles.source_quote: If gender attributes, however, are not expressive but performative, then these attributes effectively constitute the identity they are said to express or reveal.

mental_models.name: Gender as Theatrical Performance
mental_models.description: Viewing gender through the lens of theatrical acting, where a 'script' exists prior to the actor but requires the actor to actualize it. However, unlike theater where the act is de-realized ('just a play'), gender performance in real life lacks these boundaries and carries real punitive risks for deviating from the script.
mental_models.example: Comparing a transvestite on stage (who elicits applause because theatrical conventions separate the performance from reality) to a transvestite on a bus (who elicits fear or rage because there are no conventions to delimit the imaginary from the real).

mental_models.name: The Body as a Historical Situation
mental_models.description: The body is not a static, natural object or a mere factic materiality, but an active process of embodying cultural and historical possibilities. It bears meaning through dramatic expression.
mental_models.example: Understanding that one does not simply 'have' a body, but rather one 'does' one's body, materializing possibilities that are constrained by available historical conventions.

heuristics: One is not simply a body, but, in some very key sense, one does one's body.
Gender reality is real only to the extent that it is performed.

signature_quotes.text: gender is in no way a stable identity or locus of agency from which various acts proceede; rather, it is an identity tenuously constituted in time-an identity instituted through a stylized repetition of acts.
signature_quotes.context: Defining the core concept of gender performativity.

signature_quotes.text: Because there is neither an 'essence' that gender expresses or externalizes nor an objective ideal to which gender aspires; because gender is not a fact, the various acts of gender creates the idea of gender, and without those acts, there would be no gender at all.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how the performance of gender creates the illusion of its natural existence.

signature_quotes.text: Gender reality is performative which means, quite simply, that it is real only to the extent that it is performed.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the performative nature of gender reality.

signature_quotes.text: As performance which is performative, gender is an 'act,' broadly construed, which constructs the social fiction of its own psychological interiority.
signature_quotes.context: Contrasting her view with the idea that gender is a role expressing an inner self.

signature_quotes.text: Genders, then, can be neither true nor false, neither real nor apparent.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding that because gender is performative, it cannot be measured against a pre-existing 'true' identity.

anti_patterns.description: The Expressive Model of Gender (Essentialism): Believing that gender acts, postures, and gestures are expressions of a pre-existing, internal, spiritual, or psychological core.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It obscures the fact that gender is performatively constructed. Postulating a 'true' gender identity is a regulatory fiction used to control and punish those who deviate, forcing gender to comply with a false model of truth and falsity.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on the category of 'woman' as an unexamined universal presupposition in feminist theory.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It risks reproducing the very binary gender identities and conditions of oppression that feminism seeks to dismantle. It assumes 'woman' is a stable ontological category rather than questioning how the category itself is socially constructed.
